name: South Korea (Republic of Korea / 대한민국)
summary: South Korea is an East Asian civil-law unitary republic whose family-law framework operates under the Civil Act (민법) Book IV (Relatives) and Family Litigation Act 1990 (가사소송법). Parental authority (친권) is governed by Civil Act arts. 909-927; the 2005 reform shifted from hojuje (patriarchal household head) to gajokje (family register) and 2011 reform abolished hojuje. Joint exercise during marriage is the statutory default. The Supreme Court of Korea (대법원) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Court (헌법재판소) operates separate constitutional-review jurisdiction. Specialised Family Courts (가정법원) operate at first instance. Psychology profession is regulated under the Mental Health Act framework with Korean Psychological Association (KPA) operating professional standards; Korean Clinical Psychology Association (KCPA) administers the Clinical Psychologist (임상심리사) qualification. South Korea is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the chiae uichunjuui (child's best interests) standard. South Korea acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 March 2013.

statutory_framework.title: Civil Act — Parental authority (친권)
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal civil code governing parental authority. Substantially amended by 2005 reform (gajokje replacing hojuje) and 2011 reform abolishing hojuje. Joint exercise during marriage is the statutory default.

statutory_framework.title: Family Litigation Act (가사소송법)
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal procedural statute for Family Court proceedings.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Korea (대법원)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Court (헌법재판소)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Court with original jurisdiction over constitutional review and constitutional complaints.

professional_regulators.name: Korean Psychological Association (KPA / 한국심리학회)
professional_regulators.role: Peak professional association for psychologists in Korea.

professional_regulators.name: Korean Clinical Psychology Association (KCPA)
professional_regulators.role: Specialist association administering the Clinical Psychologist (임상심리사) qualification under the Mental Health Act framework.

anonymisation_convention: Korean family-law decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Civil Act (민법) enacted
key_developments.description: Federal Civil Act enacted 22 February 1958, effective 1 January 1960, in German-Japanese civil-law tradition with continental-style codified family-law provisions in Book IV (Relatives) — the substantive foundation for Korean family-law jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Civil Act Family-Law revision — gender-equality first wave
key_developments.description: First major Family-Law revision enacted 31 December 1977, effective 1 January 1979, expanding spouse-inheritance rights, introducing parental-authority joint-exercise during marriage, and beginning the gradual gender-equality reform stream within the hojuje framework.

key_developments.title: Family Litigation Act + Civil Act Family-Law revision (1989)
key_developments.description: Family Litigation Act (가사소송법) enacted 13 December 1990, effective 1 July 1991, codifying specialised Family-Court procedure. Concurrently, Civil Act Family-Law revision enacted 13 December 1989, effective 1 January 1991, substantially advancing gender-equality in marital-property, parental-authority, and divorce-procedure provisions — the major pre-2005 reform laying the groundwork for hojuje abolition.

key_developments.title: Constitutional Court 2 February 2005 hojuje unconstitutionality + Civil Act reform
key_developments.description: Constitutional Court Decision 2001Hun-Ka9 etc. of 3 February 2005 declared the patriarchal household-head system (hojuje 호주제) unconstitutional under Constitution Art. 36(1) (family-life dignity and gender-equality). Civil Act revision enacted 31 March 2005 replacing hojuje with the new family-register system (gajokje 가족관계등록제) — landmark reform anchored in the Constitutional Court decision.

key_developments.title: Hojuje abolition effective + gajokje implementation
key_developments.description: Hojuje formally abolished and gajokje (family-relationship register) implemented effective 1 January 2008 under the Act on Registration of Family Relations (가족관계의 등록 등에 관한 법률) enacted 31 May 2007 — the operational transition to the modern Korean family-register system.

key_developments.title: Multicultural Families Support Act + cross-border-family-law foundation
key_developments.description: Multicultural Families Support Act (다문화가족지원법) enacted 21 March 2008 + effective 22 September 2008 — substantively significant Korean cross-border-family-law framework foundation. Substantive Multicultural Families Support Act framework establishing legal-protection + integration-support + child-protection framework for cross-border-marriage families + relevant to subsequent Hague Convention 1980 accession 2013 + 2024 Supreme Court friendly-parent + best-interest jurisprudence. Foundation for Korean cross-border-parental-abduction + cross-border-PA-cases framework given rapid growth in cross-border marriages (~20% of Korean marriages 2010s).

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: South Korea acceded to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction 1980 — instrument deposited 13 December 2012, effective 1 March 2013, with the Korean Ministry of Justice designated as Central Authority — placing Korea in the Hague-Convention-1980 cluster with Japan (2014 accession) and other East Asian states.

key_developments.title: Act on Special Cases concerning the Punishment of Specific Sexual Crimes (revised) + Children's Welfare Act (revised)
key_developments.description: Children's Welfare Act (아동복지법) substantively revised 2014 expanding child-protection mechanisms, mandatory-reporting obligations, and recognition of psychological abuse — supplementing the Civil Act parental-authority framework with explicit child-protection register.

key_developments.title: Constitutional Court 28 June 2018 — Art. 36(1) family-life-dignity expansion
key_developments.description: Constitutional Court continued to develop the Art. 36(1) family-life-dignity and gender-equality jurisprudence post-hojuje-abolition — addressing residual patriarchal-doctrinal elements in family-relations-register and post-divorce family-name provisions.

key_developments.title: Civil Act art. 915 child-corporal-punishment provision abolition
key_developments.description: Civil Act art. 915 (parental authority including 'necessary disciplinary measures') substantively revised by Act 17905 of 26 January 2021 — explicitly eliminating the legal basis for parental corporal punishment of children. Korea became the 62nd country globally to prohibit all corporal punishment of children, aligning with CRC General Comment No. 8 (2006).

key_developments.title: Family Litigation Act revisions + Mediation framework reinforcement
key_developments.description: Family Litigation Act (가사소송법) revisions 2022-2023 strengthening family-court mediation (조정) framework under Civil Act arts. 909-927 and refining post-divorce visitation enforcement (면접교섭) procedures. Substantively significant procedural-reform framework — operationally affecting subsequent Family Court adjudication of contact-refusal cases with substantive best-interest-of-the-child analysis. Establishes trajectory toward 2024 Supreme Court Family Chamber friendly-parent jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Supreme Court Family Chamber — friendly-parent + best-interest jurisprudence
key_developments.description: Supreme Court and Family Courts continue to develop best-interest-of-the-child jurisprudence under Civil Act arts. 909-927 in custody disputes including allegations of one-parent obstruction of the other-parent relationship — substantive analysis within best-interest doctrine without adopting the 'parental alienation' label as a distinct doctrinal term.

structural_findings: South Korea operates a civil-law framework in German-Japanese codified tradition with significant 1977-1989-2005-2008 gender-equality + patriarchal-system reform stream — Constitutional Court 2005 hojuje-unconstitutionality + 2007 gajokje statute + 2008 abolition-effective, structurally aligning South Korea with modern East Asian family-law systems.
Hague-1980-Convention accession 2013 places South Korea in the Hague-1980 East-Asian cluster alongside Japan (2014 accession) — within the broader Hague-Convention cluster.

Civil Act art. 915 child-corporal-punishment provision abolition 2021 places Korea among the 62 countries globally to prohibit all corporal punishment of children — Constitutional Court Art. 36(1) family-life-dignity expansion stream.

Constitutional Court active in family-law-constitutional-review — 2005 hojuje-unconstitutionality + 2018 Art. 36(1) family-life-dignity expansion + ongoing review of family-relations-register and post-divorce family-name provisions.

Psychology profession regulation operates through Mental Health Act framework + KPA + KCPA peak-body — lacks unified federal-statutory psychology regulator typical of European/Anglosphere comparators; structurally similar to China (CPS) and distinct from Taiwan (Psychologists Act 2001) and Japan (Public Psychologist Act 2017).
Specialised Family Courts under Family Litigation Act 1990 align Korea with the East-Asian specialised-Family-Court cluster (Taiwan 2012 Family Act, Japan 1948 Family Court Act).
editorial_notes: South Korea jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 5 to 10 key_developments with full Civil-Act trajectory + Constitutional Court anchor: 1958-Civil-Act-enactment + 1977-gender-equality-first-wave + 1989-1990-Family-Litigation-Act-+-Civil-Act-revision + 2005-Constitutional-Court-hojuje-unconstitutionality + 2008-hojuje-abolition-effective-gajokje-implementation + 2013-Hague-1980-accession + 2014-Children-Welfare-Act-revision + 2018-Constitutional-Court-Art-36-1-expansion + 2021-corporal-punishment-abolition + 2024-Supreme-Court-best-interest.

Civil-law framework in German-Japanese codified tradition + Civil Act Book IV (1958) + Family Litigation Act 1990 + Constitutional Court 2005 hojuje-unconstitutionality + gajokje implementation 2008 + Hague-1980 accession 2013 + corporal-punishment abolition 2021 + Mental Health Act framework + KPA/KCPA peak-body.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive best-interest-of-the-child analysis under Civil Act arts. 909-927 without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins East-Asian + civil-law + German-Japanese-civil-tradition + Hague-1980-Convention-East-Asian-cluster (with Japan) + East-Asian-specialised-Family-Court-cluster + corporal-punishment-prohibition-frontier-62 clusters within the corpus.
